source_metadata.title_raw: Anthony Saul Alperin -  A-82-015 - February 10, 1982 - Los Angeles

content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95824 · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance     Administration     Executive/Legal     Enforcement     Statements of Economic Intere
(916) 322-5662          322-5660            322-5901            322-6441           222-6444

February 10, 1982

                                                                                    82-0215

Anthony Saul Alperin
Los Angeles City
Attorney's Office
City Hall
Los Angeles, CA  90012

                                        Re:  Telephone Request for
                                             Advice Regarding Disclosure
                                             of Economic Interests

Dear Mr. Alperin:

     Thank you for your recent telephone request for advice.
This letter is to confirm and amplify our telephone advice.
In your capacity as Deputy City Attorney for the City of
Los Angeles, you are called upon to advise numerous city
officials regarding their disclosure obligations under the
Political Reform Act—/ when filing their Statements of Economic
Interest. You have asked for our advice as to the proper
disclosure required in the following circumstances:

     A public official and his/her spouse jointly own a
business (leasing real estate). What, if anything is reported
in each of the following instances:

     (1)  The business makes no profit. The public official
          and spouse receive no disbursements (salary or
          otherwise); but the business has gross income in
          excess of $500.

     (2)  The business makes a profit. It is left in the
          business' bank account; no disbursements; no salary,
          etc.; and the profit exceeds $500.

     (3)  The business makes a profit and in excess of $500
          is distributed to the public official and his/her
          spouse.

_______________________
1/Government Code Sections 81001-91014. Unless otherwise
specified, all references are to the Government Code.

Anthony Saul Alperin
February 10, 1982
Page Two


     Our response is that the reporting requirements in each
instance would be the same.  The public official would be
required to report his/her pro rata share of the gross income
of the business entity on the schedule for reporting income,
since his/her share would exceed the $250 reporting threshold.
If an individual lessee's payments to the business were so
large that the official's individual pro rata share would
meet the $10,000 threshold contained in Section 87207(b)(3),
then the lessee would also be reported on the schedule for
reporting income to a business entity.  You have asked that
we elaborate on our analysis which underlies this advice.
Our analysis has been aided by referring to our files on
previous advice given in response to similar questions.

     We read Section 82030(a) as including income of a busi-
ness entity as part of an individual's income.

     . . . Income of an individual also includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the individual owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.  . . .

                              Section 82030(a)

     Since this pro rata share of business income is part of
an individual's income, it is reportable pursuant to Section
87207(a) if it exceeds $250.  Thus a filer would report
his/her pro rata share of the gross income of any business
entity in which he/she owns a 10-percent or greater interest,
directly, indirectly or beneficially.  However, the normal
requirement that the source of that income revealed
when payments exceed $250, is modified with respect to sources
of income to the business entity.  Under Section 87207(b),
sources of income to the business entity are individually
reported only if they reach the pro rata $10,000 threshold.
Previous advice letters confirm this analysis.—/

     —/ See the Commission's Opinion, Carey, 3 FPPC Opinions
99, No. 76-017, November 3, 1977, at pages 103-104 for discus-
sion of why it is gross income.

     —3/ See Section 87207(a)(1).

     —4/ Section 87207(a) defers to Section 87207(b) as to the
reporting of the individual sources of income only, not as
to the reporting of the income.

     —5/ These advice letters also confirm that this advice
applies to all types of business entities:  corporate, partner-
ship or sole proprietorship.

Anthony Saul Alperin
February 10, 1982
Page Three


In a November 5, 1976, letter to Dr. Edward B. Feehan,
we stated:

     Basically, whenever a person owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, in
a business entity, the individual is required to disclose
his or her pro rata share of any income of the business
entity. This requirement is found in Section 82030
which defines income.

     Similar reference to the provisions of Section 82030
was made in another 1976 letter (September 21, 1976, letter
to Honorable Eugene T. Gualco). In addition, a December 22,
1976, letter to Honorable Wade N. Shifflett stated as follows:

     4. With respect to rental income, you must disclose
the gross amount received. However, you need not disclose
the name and address of any tenant unless your pro rata
share of the gross rental payments from that tenant
equals $10,000 or more during the calendar year.

     The Feehan letter (supra) was referenced specifically
in a February 7, 1977, letter to Ralph Prince, in which we
stated:

     Moreover, should the Commissioner own directly, indirectly,
or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, in
the business entity, the Commissioner would be required
to disclose his or her pro rata share of any income of
the business entity. This requirement is dictated by
Government Code §82030, which states: . . .

     Again, in 1977 (May 16, 1977), we gave this advice to
Ronald Kaldor, stating as follows:

     At the onset it should be clear that income to an indi-
vidual includes a pro rata share of the income to a
business entity in which the individual owns a 10%
interest or greater. Thus, where, as appears here, the
physician owns 100% of the corporation, income to the
corporation is income to the physician.

     Later (December 27, 1977), we wrote to Douglas A. Lewis
who was seeking advice on behalf of Dr. Black; there we
said:

Anthony Saul Alperin
February 10, 1982
Page Four


     Thus, along with Dr. Black's medical practice itself, certain patients of Dr. Black's who provide income to this business entity - the medical practice - will be a source of income to him.  However, ... only those sources of income to a business entity that pay in excess of $10,000 in fees during the applicable reporting period need be disclosed on a Statement of Economic Interests.  This conclusion is dictated by Section 87207(b)(3)....

     Again, we read Section 87207(b) as being a limitation only on the level at which individual clients or customers must be disclosed, not as a limit on disclosure of business income.  As recently as May 26, 1981, Commission staff again reiterated this analysis in internal discussions.

     You have stated that your own notes reflect telephone advice from a Commission staff attorney (January 1979) to the effect that only distributed net income from a business entity need be reported on the schedule for reporting income.  This is contrary to the advice contained in this letter and the others referred to herein.  You have asked whether you should now advise your clients to amend their past filings in order to avoid potential liability for failure to disclose gross business income on that schedule.  I have advised you that this is not necessary and that no client of yours, who actually relied upon your advice, based upon our telephone advice, will be charged with this specific violation of the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act.

     Obviously, in the future your advice should be per our analysis above.  If I can be of any further assistance to you in this regard, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
REL:plh                                 Legal Division
Enclosures

5/You have stated that you consistently have advised your clients that they must report their pro rata share of gross income from an individual client or customer, when that share is $10,000 or more, on the schedule for reporting income to a business entity.

sections.question: A public official and his/her spouse jointly own a business (leasing real estate). What, if anything is reported in each of the following instances: (1) The business makes no profit. The public official and spouse receive no disbursements (salary or otherwise); but the business has gross income in excess of $500. (2) The business makes a profit. It is left in the business' bank account; no disbursements; no salary, etc.; and the profit exceeds $500. (3) The business makes a profit and in excess of $500 is distributed to the public official and his/her spouse.
sections.conclusion: The public official would be required to report his/her pro rata share of the gross income of the business entity on the schedule for reporting income, since his/her share would exceed the $250 reporting threshold. If an individual lessee's payments to the business were so large that the official's individual pro rata share would meet the $10,000 threshold contained in Section 87207(b)(3), then the lessee would also be reported on the schedule for reporting income to a business entity.

sections.question_synthetic: A Deputy City Attorney seeks clarification on the disclosure requirements for public officials who jointly own a real estate leasing business under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding what must be reported on Statements of Economic Interest in three scenarios involving business income with varying profit distributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The reporting requirements are the same in all three instances: the official must report their pro rata share of gross income (not net profit or distributions) if it exceeds $250, and must identify individual income sources if the pro rata share from a specific source reaches $10,000. Prior advice suggesting only distributed net income need be reported was incorrect and will not result in enforcement action for those who relied on it.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with clear Q&A structure. Contains explicit QUESTION section and detailed CONCLUSION section. References multiple prior advice letters to support analysis. Includes important footnote clarifying that prior January 1979 telephone advice will not result in enforcement action.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A public official and his/her spouse jointly own a business (leasing real estate). What, if anything is reported in each of the following instances: (1) The business makes no profit. The public official and spouse receive no disbursements (salary or otherwise); but the business has gross income in excess of $500. (2) The business makes a profit. It is left in the business' bank account; no disbursements; no salary, etc.; and the profit exceeds $500. (3) The business makes a profit and in excess of $500 is distributed to the public official and his/her spouse.

CONCLUSION: The public official would be required to report his/her pro rata share of the gross income of the business entity on the schedule for reporting income, since his/her share would exceed the $250 reporting threshold. If an individual lessee's payments to the business were so large that the official's individual pro rata share would meet the $10,000 threshold contained in Section 87207(b)(3), then the lessee would also be reported on the schedule for reporting income to a business entity.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Deputy City Attorney Anthony Alperin that public officials must disclose their pro rata share of gross income from jointly-owned business entities on Statements of Economic Interest if it exceeds $250, regardless of whether the business is profitable or distributes funds, and clarifies that prior conflicting staff advice will not be enforced against those who followed it.
